coredata.dc:title: Resonant transport and negative differential resistance in the graphene and graphyne quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ye
Xie, Yuee
Ouyang, Tao
Chen, Yuanping
coredata.dc:description: The electronic transport properties of graphene and graphyne quantum dots embedded in zigzag-edged graphene nanoribbons are studied. The results show that the quasi-bound states in the quantum dots induce resonant transmission around the Fermi level in the transmission spectrums. While the resonant peaks lead to robust negative differential resistance (NDR) behaviors. Moreover, the resonant transmission and NDR behavior are very sensitive to the size of the quantum dots. As the size of quantum dots increases, the number of resonant peaks increases and shift to the Fermi level, correspondingly the NDR phenomena shift to lower bias. Compared with graphene quantum dots, graphyne quantum dots show more amazing transport properties. These interesting findings could offer useful guidelines for the design of electronics associated with resonant and NDR phenomenon.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Graphyne quantum dots
NDR
Resonant transmissions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic views of (a) GQD and (b) GYQD is sandwiched between two ZGNR electrodes. W represents the width of the nanoribbon, and parameter n/m is the number of intermediate carbon hexagonal rings in these systems.

(a) and (c) describes the transmission coefficient of 3-GQD and 3-GYQD systems; The vertical blue lines correspond to the molecular orbitals. (b) and (d) describe the DOS as a function of electron energy of 3-GQD and 3-GYQD systems. The insets refer to the localization density of states (LDOS) in real space at −0.011eV, −0.969eV and 1.37eV for 3-GQD system, and at −0.002eV, −0.9169eV and 0.634eV for 3-GYQD system. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Current–voltage (I–V) curve of the (a) 3-GQD system and (b) 3-GYQD system in the bias range [0, 3.0V].

The transmission coefficients of the 3-GYQD system at the bias voltages (a) V b=0.9, (b) 1.2, (c) 1.3 and (d) 1.9V, respectively, and the MPSH of LUMO (left inset) and LUMO+1 (right inset) of 3-GYQD under biases (b) 1.2 and (c) 1.3V. The region between the red solid lines is the bias window, the vertical dash lines correspond to the molecular orbitals, and the shaded area denotes the area inside the bias window. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The transmission spectrums of (a) 2-GQD system, (b) 3-GQD system, (c) 4-GQD system and (d) 5-GQD system, (e) 2-GYQD system, (f) 3-GYQD system, (g) 4-GYQD system and (h) 5-GYQD system. The average Fermi level is set to be zero.
Calculated currents as a function of the bias of (a) 4-GQD system, (b) 5-GQD system, (c) 4-GYQD system and (d) 5-GYQD system.

head.ce:title: Resonant transport and negative differential resistance in the graphene and graphyne quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory for Quantum Engineering and Micro–Nano Energy Technology and Institute of Physics, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, Hunan, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory for Quantum Engineering and Micro–Nano Energy Technology and Institute of Physics, Xiangtan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electronic transport properties of graphene and graphyne quantum dots embedded in zigzag-edged graphene nanoribbons are studied. The results show that the quasi-bound states in the quantum dots induce resonant transmission around the Fermi level in the transmission spectrums. While the resonant peaks lead to robust negative differential resistance (NDR) behaviors. Moreover, the resonant transmission and NDR behavior are very sensitive to the size of the quantum dots. As the size of quantum dots increases, the number of resonant peaks increases and shift to the Fermi level, correspondingly the NDR phenomena shift to lower bias. Compared with graphene quantum dots, graphyne quantum dots show more amazing transport properties. These interesting findings could offer useful guidelines for the design of electronics associated with resonant and NDR phenomenon.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, the carbon-based nano-materials had attracted much attentions. Graphene [1], a single layer of carbon atoms, has unique electronic structures and transport properties, such as giant carrier mobility [2], Klein tunneling [3,4], and anomalous quantum hall effects [5]. The two-dimensional carbon allotrope has been considered as an important material in electronics because of its excellent properties [6,7]. Recently, the research on graphene has been rapidly expanded to different types of layered carbon allotropes, i.e., graphyne [6], consisting of triple- and double-bonded carbon atoms. Four different types of graphyne, namely α-, β-, γ-, and 6,6,12-graphynes [7,8] have been identified according to the peculiar structural characteristic of triple carbon bonds (–CC–), in which γ-graphyne has been synthesized successfully [9]. The first-principles calculations have shown that graphyne exhibits small carrier effective masses and high carrier mobility like graphene [10]. Besides, graphyne also possesses rich physical properties quite different from those of graphene due to the presence of acetylenic linkages. For example, the γ-graphyne is a direct band gap semiconductor [11,12], while the Dirac cones of 6,6,12-graphyne are not perfectly conical but distorted ones that should lead to a conductance depending on the direction of the current [7]. The exotic electric properties of graphyne open new possibilities for electronic applications of carbon materials besides graphene.

By utilizing lithography techniques graphene and graphyne can be etched or cut into various shapes, such as quasi-one-dimensional nanoribbons (Q1D) [13–15] and quasi-zero-dimensional quantum dots (Q0D) [16–19]. Nanostructures based on graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) have been studied extensively and many interesting quantum transport phenomena, such as quantum resonant transmission and negative differential resistance (NDR) effect have been found [20–27]. For example, there are resonant transmissions existing in Z-, S- and L-shaped GNRs, and the resonant transport are associated with bound states [21–23]. In N-doped armchair-edge GNRs and crossed GNRs, NDR behaviors are observed in a specific range of applied bias [24,25]. Recently, NDR behaviors are also found in a designed B- and N-doped p–n junctions based on armchair-edge GNRs [26]. These fascinating physical properties have been gained spread interest because of potential applications in the future nanoelectronics devices [28,29].

Compared with the Q1D structure of graphene and graphyne nanoribbons, it is known that the quasi-zero-dimensional (Q0D) quantum dot systems [30] have stronger confinement effect than that in the quasi-one-dimensional nanoribbons. Therefore, the quantum dots systems should conceive more remarkable quantum effect because of their three-dimensional confinement [31,32]. Using graphene side gates, tunable Coulomb blockade was demonstrated in the graphene quantum dots (GQD) [33]. Quantum-confinement effects in the form of excited states and co-tunneling lines were confirmed [34], and excited states were observed as well in the GQD systems [35,36]. However, previous studies were mainly in the experimental research, less studies focus on the resonant transmissions and NDR in the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and graphyne quantum dots (GYQDs) until now, especially in the latter systems.

In this paper, we studied the transport properties of GQDs and GYQDs, which is sandwiched between two semi-infinite zigzag-edge GNR electrodes. The results show that the quasi-bound states in the quantum dots induce drastic resonant transmissions and NDR phenomena. The number of resonant peaks increases and the peaks shifts to the Fermi level as the size of QDs increases, which leads to the NDR behavior appearing at lower bias. Due to the stronger quantum confinement effect, the QD systems exhibit sequential NDR behaviors while most systems based on nanoribbons just show single NDR behavior. The observed sequential NDR behavior has a wide range of applications such as successive switching process [37], multiple-valued memories [38], analog-to-digital converters [39], and multiple-valued logics [40]. Comparing the GQD and GYQD systems, we find that in the GYQD systems the resonant peaks are sharper and more NDR peaks appear at lower bias region.

The model is illustrated in Fig. 1, which is divided into three regions, i.e., two (left and right) semi-infinite electrodes and a scattering region. We use zigzag graphene nanoribbons (ZGNRs) as the left and right electrodes. The width of ZGNRs is W=4 (which consists of four zigzag carbon chains). The scattering region contains the quantum dots and four surface layers. Fig. 1(a) is a diamond graphene quantum dots (GQDs) embedded in the two semi-infinite electrodes. The size of the quantum dot systems are defined by n-GQD where n is the number of intermediate carbon hexagonal ring. Fig. 1(b) is an m-GYQD (m is the number of intermediate carbon ring) embedded in two semi-infinite electrodes. All carbon atoms at edges are saturated with hydrogen atoms (white) for a better structural and electronic stability.

The geometric optimization and electronic transport properties are all calculated by a developed ab initio software package Atomistix ToolKit (ATK) [41], which is based on the pseudopotential density-functional theory combined with the none-quilibrium Greens functions. In the calculation, the exchange-correlation potential is approximated within the generalized gradient approximation using PBE function (GGA.PBE) [42]. The geometry optimizations have been relaxed with sufficient vacuum spaces (minimum of 10Å) to prevent the interactions with periodic images of the structure. The electrostatic potentials were determined on a real space grid with a mesh cutoff energy of 150Ry. The criteria of convergence for total energy and Hellman–Feynman forces were 10−5 eV and 0.05eV/Å, respectively. Double-zeta double-polarized basis sets of local numerical orbitals were employed in our systems. The Brillouin zone has been sampled with (1, 1, 100) points within the Monkhorst Pack k-point sampling scheme. The calculational process for ATK program is briefly described as follows: At first, self-energies of left and right electrodes are obtained as given a self-consistent potential depending on bias. Then the electron density can be obtained from the Green׳s function of the center scattering region. Next, the Hamiltonian making use of evaluating Green׳s function can be provided. Through self-consistent iterative scheme, the electron density can be finally computed. Once the electron density for the system is known, in principle, all physical quantities can be achieved such as transmission coefficient, density of state (DOS), molecular orbitals, molecular projected self-consistent Hamiltonian (MPSH), and current [43], etc. The transmission coefficient T ( E ,
V
b
) is presented as
(1)
T ( E ,
V b
) = T r
Im
∑ L
( E ,
V b
)
G R
( E ,
V b
) Im
∑ R
( E ,
V b
)
G A
( E ,
V b
)
where
G
R ( A )
is the retarded (advanced) Green׳s function of the center scattering region. The nonlinear current through the device is calculated using the Landauer–Büttiker formula
(2)
I = ( 2 e / h )
∫
μ
L
μ
R
T ( E ,
V
b
)
d E
where
μ
L / R
are electrochemical potentials of the left and right electrodes [44]. With the applied bias potential
V
b
, the difference in the chemical potentials are given by e
V
b
, and we use
μ
L
(
V

) =
μ
L
( 0 ) − e
V
b
/ 2 and
μ
R
(
V
b
) =
μ
R
( 0 ) + e
V
b
/ 2 . The energy region of the transmission spectrum that contributes to the current is referred to as the bias window.

In Fig. 2(a) and (b), we show the transmission coefficient and density of states (DOS) as a function of electron energy for the 3-GQD system (see Fig. 1(a)). One can find that a large band gap (about 2.45eV) exists in the transmission spectrum, and some transmission peaks appear far from the Fermi level. Each transmission peak corresponds to a DOS peak. Electrons in the peaks are tightly confined around the quantum dots (see inset in Fig. 2(a)), indicating that these peaks correspond to quasi-bound states in the quantum dots. Except these DOS peaks, one can find that there are two very sharp DOS peaks around E=±1.0eV and a sharp peak at the Fermi level. The left inset in Fig. 2(b) shows that the states corresponding to the former two peaks are confined in the quantum dot completely, therefore they are quantum bound states with an infinite life time and cannot induce resonant transmission. While the right inset in Fig. 2(b) indicates that the state at the Fermi level are confined at the electrodes, which cannot lead to transmission either.

The transmission coefficient and DOS profiles for 3-GYQD system are shown in Fig. 2(c) and (d), respectively. Comparing Fig. 2(c) with (a), one can find that the energy gap of 3-GYQD system is smaller (about 1.12eV) than that of 3-GQD system, meanwhile denser and sharper resonant peaks exist around the Fermi level. The different resonant transport between GQD and GYQD is originated from the different molecular energy levels of the two QDs (see the short lines in Fig. 2(a) and (c)). Molecular energy levels of 3-GYQD are denser and closer to the Fermi level. In addition, The DOS and LDOS of GYQD show that the resonant transmissions are also induced by the quasi-bound states localized in the quantum dots.

In Fig. 3(a) and (b), we compare the current–voltage (I–V) characteristic of 3-GQD and 3-GYQD systems within the bias range [0, 3.0V]. For the 3-GQD system, the current is zero until a threshold bias 1.80V because its big band gap. When the bias voltage is larger than the threshold bias voltage, the current rapidly increases with bias voltage up to 2.60V and then decreases in the range [2.60, 2.70V]. In this case NDR is observed. The peak-to-valley ratio (PVR) (the ratio of the peak current I
p to the valley current I
v ) is 1.2. For the 3-GYQD system, the threshold bias voltage is relatively smaller. The current starts increasing from voltage 0.90V and decreases rapidly in the range [1.2, 1.3V], which shows an NDR behavior with a very high peak-to-valley ratio (PVR=43.6). Moreover, with the further increase of voltage more NDR peak–valley pairs appear in the higher voltage regions [1.9, 2.0V] and [2.3, 2.4V], i.e., multiple NDR phenomena are observed. These results indicate that the GYQD system is better than the GQD system because the former has smaller threshold bias voltage and more prominent NDR effect.

I–V characteristics of QD systems are determined by their transmission properties under bias voltage, while the transmission across QD is related with the molecular energy levels. To explain the NDR behaviors in the QDs, in Fig. 4 we display the transmission coefficients and the molecular-projected self-consistent Hamiltonians (MPSH) for 3-GYQD system at different bias voltage. When the bias voltage is 0.9V, there are no molecular levels in the bias window (see the red solid lines), thus no transmission and also no current transport across the GYQD. With the increase of voltage, the molecular levels will shift to low energy gradually and the quantum states corresponding to the levels will change. When the bias voltage is increased to V
b =1.2V, three molecular levels (LOMO, LOMO+1 and LOMO+2) shift into the bias window (see Fig. 4(b)). Under bias voltage, the MPSH of LUMO is localized, therefore this molecular level cannot induce resonant transmission. While the two molecular levels of LUMO+1 and LUMO+2 induce a resonant peak, and thus leads to a big current at V
b =1.2V. As the bias voltage is increased to 1.3V, the two states corresponding to LUMO+1 and LOMO+2 evolve into states like that at LUMO, where the transport channel is completely suppressed, as shown in the insets of Fig. 4(c). In this case the resonant peak in the bias window in Fig. 4(b) disappears. Correspondingly, the current across the QD decreases and the NDR behavior appears. With the further increase of bias voltage, one can see that another molecular levels shift into the bias window (see Fig. 4(d)). The new levels induce resonant peaks and then lead to the increase of current, which is the start of new NDR behavior. One can see from Fig. 4 that the NDR behaviors in the QD systems are correlated tightly with the resonant peaks in their transmission spectrums. So, the different transmission spectrums of the two QD systems result in different NDR phenomena.

The transport properties of nanostructures are very sensitive to the structural size. The effect of the QD size on the transmission spectrum of QD systems is shown in Fig. 5. Fig. 5(a)–(d) displays the transmission spectrums at zero bias for the n-GQD systems with n=2–5, respectively. We can see that, with the size increases, the band gap becomes smaller, this is reasonable due to less confinement for samples with larger size can strength the electron transport. Meanwhile more and sharper resonant peaks shift to the Fermi level. As a comparison, the transmission coefficient for m-GYQD systems with m=2, 3, 4 and 5 are plotted in Fig. 5(e)–(h), respectively. Similar phenomenon is found in these QD systems. Moreover, the resonant peaks in the GYQD systems are denser and sharper. The above results indicate that these QDs systems could produce very significant resonant transmissions phenomenon and can be tuned by their sizes.

Correspondingly, Fig. 6 shows the current as a function of the bias voltages, (a) and (b) for 4-, 5-GQD systems; (c) and (d) for 4-, 5-GYQD systems, respectively. In Fig. 6(a) and (b), one can observe that the GQD systems show the NDR behaviors at the specific bias regions, such as, the 4-GQD system at around 1.9V, the 5-GQD system at aound 1.6V and 1.9V. The threshold bias voltage and the bias voltage value of NDR decrease as the GQD size increases. Then the current–voltage (I–V) curves for the 4-GYQD system and 5-GYQD system are shown in Fig. 6(c) and (d), respectively. More NDR peaks are observed, and the voltage values of NDR in the GYQD systems are smaller than those in the GQD systems. So we can conclude that the NDR can appear at the lower bias voltage and there are more remarkable NDR peaks for m-GYQD systems. The threshold bias voltage and the NDR behaviors can be modulated by the size of the QDs.

In summary, we have investigated the transport properties of n-GQD/m-GYQD systems by using the first-principles calculations. It is found that the resonant transmissions are induced by the quasi-bound states confined in the QDs, thus the current–voltage characteristics of these QD systems which exhibit several representative peaks of multiple NDR. The resonant peaks and NDR behaviors can be tuned by the size of QDs. As the size of QDs increases, the number of resonant peaks increases and shift to the Fermi level, this result in NDR behaviors that appeared at lower bias. In addition, comparing the two carbon-based QD systems, GYQD systems possess more amazing electronic transport properties for more robust resonant transmission and NDR behavior appeared at lower bias. These results could provide useful applications based on the graphene or graphyne quantum dots in electronics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51176161, 51376005, 11204262 and 11304262).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: T. Waho, M. Yamamoto, in: Proceedings of the 27th International Symposium on Multiple-Valued Logic, 1997, p. 35.
